excerpt: Sincerity (ikhlāṣ) is to perform an action purely for Allah's sake, without expectation of reward or compensation, with intentions entirely purified of worldly gain and personal interest.
The Imāms, as guardians and protectors of the believers, have demonstrated throughout history that they serve their masters with complete sincerity, seeking no recompense and voicing no complaint.
The Commander of the Faithful declared in supplication: 'O Allah, I worship Thee not from desire for Thy Paradise nor fear of Thy Hell, but because Thou art worthy of worship.'
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler); ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib (speaker)

excerpt: Sincerity arises when the believer surrenders all stratagems of the soul and all strength of the body, declaring with complete conviction: 'There is no stratagem and no strength save in Allah, the Most High, the Mighty.'

Even the knowledgeable astrologer, though possessed of full understanding of calamities to come, places trust in Allah and believes that only He can avert evil if He wills—this is the hallmark of true sincerity.

The wise person trusts in his intellect and only afterward turns to Allah when intellect fails; the knower trusts in Allah first and returns all stratagems and strength to Allah, then begins his work. For this reason the knower remains calm in tribulation while the wise person panics.
source_location_hint: Section on Difference Between Wise and Arif

excerpt: Those who trust in Allah are content with His decree, knowing that He has granted them knowledge while their enemies possess wealth—this is a blessing of such magnitude that it surpasses all worldly advantage.
source_work: Rasa'il Ahmadiyyah (Epistles) and teachings of Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq
source_author: Imām Ahmad ibn ʿAbdullāh / Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq

excerpt: No person can taste the sweetness of faith unless indifferent to all worldly pleasures. The pious soul becomes endowed with wisdom and knowledge of the Hereafter, turning away from worldly allurments to the gardens of Allah.
source_location_hint: Section on Piety and Trust in Allah

excerpt: Afflictions are of two kinds: those resulting from one's own misdeeds, which befall all humanity, and those wherein one is tested and spiritual ranks elevated, which are reserved for Allah's special servants.

For the truly patient ones, Allah declares: 'Obey Allah and His Messenger, and be not among those who lose heart. Be patient—surely Allah is with the patient.' This assurance strengthens the resolve of those who endure with faith.

The story of the blind, paralyzed elder who remained grateful despite all affliction demonstrates the second type of patience—ordinary endurance without complaint. Yet even this extraordinary patience is exceeded by the sublime patience of those who find joy in hardship for Allah's sake.
source_author: Imām Ahmad ibn ʿAbdullāh / Dhū al-Nūn al-Miṣrī
source_location_hint: Section on The Patient Ones and Their Ranks

excerpt: Those closest to Allah endure the severest trials. Ibrāhīm and Ismāʿīl exemplify the sublime patience of those who possess the power to remove their difficulties but sacrifice their relief for Allah's pleasure, offering themselves joyfully for martyrdom.
source_location_hint: Section on The Third Type of Patience

excerpt: The Prophet ﷺ distinguished: 'The believer is tested; the disbeliever is punished.' Every calamity befalling the believer aims at purification and elevation of rank, while misfortune upon the disbeliever is punishment for evil deeds.
source_author: Imām Ahmad ibn ʿAbdullāh / Prophet Muḥammad ﷺ

excerpt: Allah has made the soul vulnerable to pain so that it may protect the body from corruption. The soul's capacity to feel pain is a divine wisdom ensuring the body's preservation in this world.
source_location_hint: Section on Pain Upon the Soul

excerpt: The supreme benefit of patience is freedom—it removes the yoke of desires from one's neck and creates wealth and independence of spirit. Only those who love freedom can attain true patience.

When Sulaymān received the kingdom and dominion over all creatures, he recognized that this was a grace from his Lord—a test to determine whether he would be grateful or ungrateful. His gratitude benefits only himself, while ingratitude affects only himself; for his Lord is Rich and Bountiful.

Those who, when afflicted with calamity, say: 'To Allah we belong, and to Him we shall return'—they exemplify the believer's response to hardship, demonstrating trust and resignation to the divine will.

The disbeliever attributes all blessings to his own ability and knowledge, relying upon means while turning from his Lord. When calamity strikes, he blames Allah and becomes disgusted with mercy, saying that afflictions come from Allah as punishment rather than purification.
source_location_hint: Section on Trust of the Disbeliever

excerpt: The Prophet ﷺ said: 'If patience were created as a human, he would be a righteous man.' Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq explained that patience exemplifies the Imām as Trustee, who develops into the Imām Established—as Salmān al-Fārsī, the Imām as Trustee of the Prophet's era, progressed in the chain of Imāms.
source_author: Imām Ahmad ibn ʿAbdullāh / Imām Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq

excerpt: Allah declared to the Prophet ﷺ: 'And indeed the Hereafter is better for thee than the present.' Each thing is bound to its end, and what comes at the end surpasses what comes first—the Hereafter far exceeds the world.
